rationale: The citing paper discusses Bagchi and Ince (2016) with a specific empirical claim, rather than only listing it in a general literature set. It uses that claim - that high-likelihood forecasts are seen as more accurate and trustworthy than low-likelihood forecasts - to support a core theoretical argument about probabilistic distance and perceived instability/diagnosticity of numeric information. This role is part of hypothesis development, so the citation functions substantively in conceptual framing.
relation_description: The citing paper relies on Bagchi and Ince (2016) as prior evidence that forecast likelihood levels shape perceived accuracy and trustworthiness. It uses this evidence to ground its argument that greater probabilistic distance should reduce perceived diagnosticity of right-side digits in separated numbers.
